era_context: Transition from the Old Kingdom to the First Intermediate Period in Egypt; Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin in its final years; Indus Valley Civilization in mature phase. This year marks a major political transition in Egypt and the beginning of a period of fragmentation and decentralization.

events.title: End of the Sixth Dynasty and beginning of the First Intermediate Period in Egypt
events.description: The Sixth Dynasty of Egypt ends, traditionally dated to c. 2184 BCE, though some scholars place it c. 2150 BCE. Pepi II's death (or the end of his reign) marks the transition to the First Intermediate Period (c. 2180–2055 BCE), a period of political fragmentation and decentralization. The centralized authority of the Old Kingdom collapses, and Egypt fragments into competing regional power bases controlled by provincial governors (nomarchs). The Seventh Dynasty, which follows, is poorly attested and may have been very brief or largely legendary. This transition is marked by a decline in monumental construction, the rise of provincial autonomy, and the emergence of competing dynasties in Upper and Lower Egypt.
key_figures: Pepi II Neferkare (Last pharaoh of the Sixth Dynasty)
Nomarchs of Upper Egypt (Ankhtifi, Harkhuf, and others)

sources.name: Manetho's Aegyptiaca (preserved in Josephus and Eusebius)
Archaeological evidence of decline in royal monument construction

events.certainty_note: The end of the Sixth Dynasty and the beginning of the First Intermediate Period are well-attested in the archaeological record and nomarch inscriptions. However, the exact date is debated; traditional chronology places it c. 2184 BCE, but some scholars propose c. 2150 BCE or even c. 2200 BCE. The year 2150 BCE is assigned by convention as a plausible date for this transition.

events.title: Akkadian Empire: final years under Naram-Sin
events.description: Naram-Sin's reign is approaching its end (traditional date c. 2218 BCE). The Akkadian Empire is showing signs of severe strain: rebellions in peripheral regions are becoming more frequent, the cost of military campaigns is depleting resources, and there are indications of climate stress (possibly drought) affecting agricultural production. The empire's collapse will occur within a generation, likely due to a combination of internal rebellion, external invasion (possibly by Gutians from the Zagros), and environmental stress.

sources.name: Paleoclimatic evidence suggesting drought c. 2200 BCE

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-attested in contemporary inscriptions. The year 2150 BCE is assigned by convention within his reign (c. 2254–2218 BCE). The causes of the Akkadian collapse are debated among scholars.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization: mature phase and trade networks
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization is in its mature phase (c. 2600–1900 BCE). Urban centres like Harappa and Mohenjo-daro are fully developed and functioning. Trade networks with Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia are active. The civilization is characterized by planned urban layouts, advanced drainage systems, standardized weights and measures, and an undeciphered script. The civilization shows no evidence of large-scale warfare or fortifications, suggesting a relatively peaceful society or one organized on non-militaristic principles.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Harappa and Mohenjo-daro

events.certainty_note: The Indus Valley Civilization's existence and chronology are well-established through archaeology. The year 2150 BCE is assigned by convention within the mature phase (c. 2600–1900 BCE).

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the end of the Sixth Dynasty and the beginning of the First Intermediate Period is debated. Traditional chronology (based on the Turin King List and Manetho) places it c. 2184 BCE, but some scholars propose c. 2150 BCE, and others argue for c. 2200 BCE. The causes of the Old Kingdom's collapse are debated: some scholars emphasize internal political fragmentation, while others emphasize climate stress (drought) or external invasion. The Akkadian Empire's collapse is similarly debated; some scholars argue for a rapid collapse c. 2200 BCE due to drought and Gutian invasion, while others propose a more gradual decline over several decades.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 2150 BCE is sparse and dominated by elite sources. Egyptian sources are biased toward provincial elites and later compilations (Turin King List, Manetho); contemporary sources are limited to nomarch inscriptions and archaeological evidence. Mesopotamian sources are similarly elite-focused. The Indus Valley record is purely archaeological. The year 2150 BCE is assigned as a plausible date for the transition from the Old Kingdom to the First Intermediate Period in Egypt, but this dating is uncertain and debated. The events listed are based on broader patterns and assigned to this year by convention.

graph_edges.note: The First Intermediate Period begins; Egypt fragments into competing regional powers
Naram-Sin's reign ends; Akkadian collapse follows within ~30 years
Both Egypt and Mesopotamia experience major political transitions around this time
